perturbation.original_text: DISABILITY shall mean mental or physical illness or condition rendering Nantz incapable of fulfilling the services to be provided by him under this Agreement for a continuous period of at least 60 days.
perturbation.changed_text: DISABILITY shall mean any absence from work, regardless of the reason, lasting longer than 3 days.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Disability' aligns with standard contractual language. The modified definition broadens it significantly, potentially conflicting with California's definition of disability under the Fair Employment and Housing Act (FEHA) and the California Family Rights Act (CFRA).
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Fair Employment and Housing Act (FEHA) and California Family Rights Act (CFRA)
perturbation.law_explanation: California law defines disability more specifically and provides employees with rights and protections related to reasonable accommodation. This broad definition of 'Disability' as any absence longer than 3 days may not trigger FEHA/CFRA protections but would still allow contract termination, potentially undermining employee rights under state law.

Discrimination Prohibited [12940 - 12957] ( Chapter 6 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 992. ) ARTICLE 1. Unlawful Practices, Generally [12940 - 12954] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 992. ) 12940. It is an unlawful employment practice, unless based upon a bona fide occupational qualification, or, except where based upon applicable security regulations established by the United States or the State of California: (a) For an employer, because of the race, religious creed, color, national origin, ancestry, physical disability, mental disability, reproductive health decisionmaking, medical condition, genetic information, marital status, sex, gender, gender identity, gender expression, age, sexual orientation, or veteran or military status of any person, to refuse to hire or employ the person or to refuse to select the person for a training program leading to employment, or to bar or to discharge the person from employment or f
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Disability' as any absence longer than 3 days, which contradicts the typical understanding and legal definitions of disability, particularly in the context of employment law. The original definition aligned with a more standard contractual understanding of disability impacting one's ability to perform services, while the modified version broadens it significantly. Scraped Law Snippet 1 from the California Government Code, GOV 12940(a), defines unlawful employment practices related to discrimination based on 'physical disability' or 'mental disability'. By defining disability as any absence longer than 3 days, the changed text could potentially allow for termination of employment under circumstances that would not be considered a disability under FEHA, thus undermining the protections afforded by the statute. The modified text creates a clear contradiction because it creates an obligation that falls outside the scope of legally protected disabilities, potentially leading to discriminatory practices.

perturbation.original_text: 7. EMPLOYEE STATUS. Nantz will be a full time Ashworth employee rather than an independent contractor.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. EMPLOYEE STATUS. Nantz will be considered a part-time employee for tax purposes, but shall receive benefits equivalent to a full-time employee.
perturbation.explanation: This statement creates a contradiction. Classifying someone as 'part-time' for tax purposes while providing full-time benefits can lead to issues with tax compliance and potentially violate IRS regulations regarding employee classification and benefits eligibility.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Internal Revenue Code (IRC) regulations on employee classification and benefits
perturbation.law_explanation: The IRS has specific guidelines on classifying employees and providing benefits. Treating someone as part-time for tax purposes while giving them full-time benefits can trigger audits and penalties if it's determined they are misclassified, violating tax laws and regulations related to employee benefits.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Independent contractor defined | Internal Revenue Service Skip to main content Independent contractor defined English Español 中文 (繁體) 한국어 Русский Tiếng Việt More In File Individuals Businesses and self-employed Business tax account Employer ID numbers Business taxes Business structures Operating a business Self-employed Independent contractors Gig workers Small business Large business Charities and nonprofits International taxpayers Governmental liaisons Federal, state and local governments Indian tribal governments Tax exempt bonds Taxpayer identification numbers (TIN) People such as doctors, dentists, veterinarians, lawyers, accountants, contractors, subcontractors, public stenographers, or auctioneers who are in an independent trade, business, or profession in which they offer their services to the general public are generally independent contractors. However, whether these people are independent contractors or employees depends on the facts in each case. The general rule is that an individual is an independent contractor if the person for whom the services are performed has the right to control or direct only the result of the work and not what will be done and how it will be done. If you are an independent contractor, then you are self-employed. The earnings of a person who is working as an independent contractor are subject to self-employment tax . To find out what your tax obligations are, visit the Self-Employed Individuals Tax Center . Generally, the person for whom the services are performed must report payments to independent contractors on Form 1099-NEC, Nonemployee Compensation , in box 1. You are not an independent contractor if you perform services that can be controlled by an employer (what will be done and how it will be done). This applies even if you are given freedom of action. What matters is that the employer has the legal right to control the details of how the services are performed. If an employer-employee relationship exists (regardless of what the relationship is called), then you are not an independent contractor and your earnings are generally not subject to self-employment tax. However, your earnings as an employee may be subject to FICA (social security and Medicare tax) and income tax withholding. Generally, earnings of an employee are reported on Form W-2, Wage and Tax Statement . Refer to the page, Independent Contractor (Self-Employed) or Employee? or Publication 15-A, Employer’s Supplement Tax Guide , for more information. Related Topics Reporting payments to independent contractor Estimated taxes Backup withholding Topic no. 307, Backup withholding Page Last Reviewed or Updated: 02-Jun-2025 Share Facebook Twitter Linkedin Print
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a contradiction by designating Nantz as a 'part-time' employee for tax purposes while providing 'full-time' benefits. This contradicts IRS guidelines regarding employee classification, which can lead to misclassification and potential tax issues. Snippet 2, from the IRS, states that whether someone is an independent contractor or employee 'depends on the facts in each case.' It suggests that if an employer has the 'right to control the details of how the services are performed' an employer-employee relationship exists and the earnings are subject to FICA. By explicitly stating part-time status for tax purposes, the contract deviates from standard employment classifications that determine tax obligations. Snippet 1, 26 USC 3121, defines 'wages' as 'all remuneration for employment, including the cash value of all remuneration (including benefits) paid in any medium other than cash'. This definition is directly impacted by how the individual is classified as an employee. If 'part-time' status is incorrectly applied to avoid certain tax responsibilities, it contradicts the intent of defining what constitutes wages for tax purposes. The designation of 'part-time for tax purposes' with full-time benefits creates ambiguity and potential non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: 25. GOVERNING LAW. This Agreement and its formation, operation and performance shall be governed, construed, performed, and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: 25. GOVERNING LAW. This Agreement and its formation, operation and performance shall be governed, construed, performed, and enforced in accordance with principles of equity, irrespective of California law.
perturbation.explanation: This statement explicitly contradicts the chosen governing law. By stating the agreement will be enforced according to 'principles of equity' rather than the 'laws of the State of California', the contract introduces ambiguity and potential unenforceability. While equity may inform judicial decisions, it cannot supersede statutory law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law and Conflict of Laws Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1635 states that contracts are to be interpreted according to the language used, which implies adherence to legal statutes and case law. Substituting 'principles of equity' attempts to bypass established legal frameworks, creating a conflict and potentially rendering the governing law clause invalid.

(Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS - Sections 1635-1663 :: California Civil Code :: 2005 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2005 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 California Civil Code Sections 1635-1663 TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS CIVIL CODE SECTION 1635-1663 1635. All contracts, whether public or private, are to be interpreted by the same rules, except as otherwise provided by this Code. 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. 1637. For the purpose of ascertaining the intention of the parties to a contract, if otherwise doubtful, the rules given in this Chapter are to be applied. 1638. The language of a contract is to govern its interpretation, if the language is clear and explicit, and does not involve an absurdity. 1639. When a contract is reduced to writing, the intention of the parties is to be ascertained from the writing alone, if possible; subject, however, to the other provisions of this Title. 1640. When, through fraud, mistake, or accident, a written contract fails to express the real intention of the parties, such intention is to be regarded, and the erroneous parts of the writing disregarded. 1641. The whole of a contract is to be taken together, so as to give effect to every part, if reasonably practicable, each clause helping to interpret the other. 1642. Several contracts relating to the same matters, between the same parties, and made as parts of substantially one transaction, are to be taken together. 1643. A contract must receive such an interpretation as will make it lawful, operative, definite, reasonable, and capable of being carried into effect, if it can be done without violating the intention of the parties. 1644. perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly contradicts the original governing law. The original text states that the agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of California, while the changed text states that the agreement will be enforced according to 'principles of equity, irrespective of California law.' This creates a direct conflict, as the contract explicitly deviates from the established legal framework of California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS PART 2. CONTRACTS TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS 1635., states that all contracts are to be interpreted by the same rules, except as otherwise provided by this Code. The changed text attempts to bypass these established rules by invoking 'principles of equity' instead, thereby creating a legal contradiction.
